refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both directions of communication, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Instrument telemetry ingestion, processing, transmission, operator interface rendering, command encoding, transmission, and actuator response. Collect p95 latency across representative operational scenarios (normal, degraded network). Compare against 150 ms threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, diagnostics) and transmit to remote operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall receive operator commands and transmit to vehicle actuators with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (latency, packet loss, link failure) to operator and safety services within 50 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall implement failsafe behavior (e.g., controlled stop, safe state) if end-to-end latency exceeds 200 ms for >2 seconds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Dedicated low-latency network infrastructure (private 5G or dedicated fiber) with edge computing at vehicle and operator station; minimize hops and processing delay.
DP-B: Optimized software architecture with real-time prioritization, lock-free data structures, and hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding; use public internet with QoS tagging.
DP-C: Hybrid approach: dedicated network backbone for critical telemetry/commands; public internet for non-critical telemetry (video, diagnostics); dual-path redundancy with automatic failover.
DP-D: Cloud-based teleoperation relay with edge caching and predictive latency compensation; accept higher latency with operator-side prediction and smoothing.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid dual-path architecture with dedicated low-latency backbone for critical telemetry and commands, supplemented by public internet for non-critical data, with automatic failover and latency monitoring.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation latency to network infrastructure availability (dedicated backbone must be provisioned and maintained).
DP-C couples latency to software real-time scheduling (edge processing must be deterministic).
DP-C couples failover behavior to safety services (must coordinate safe state transitions).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are acceptable because they are explicit and manageable: network provisioning is a known project constraint, real-time scheduling is a standard embedded systems practice, and safety coordination is required by high criticality. DP-C avoids the coupling of DP-A (infrastructure dependency alone) and DP-B (software complexity without infrastructure support). DP-D introduces unacceptable coupling to operator cognitive load (prediction/smoothing).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dedicated network backbone (5G private or fiber) can be provisioned at all operational sites within project budget and timeline.
Assumption: Vehicle and operator station hardware support real-time processing (deterministic scheduling, low-jitter timers).
Assumption: Operator training includes failover behavior and latency degradation awareness.
Risk: If dedicated backbone is unavailable at a site, system falls back to public internet only; latency may exceed 150 ms, triggering failsafe.
Risk: Software real-time scheduling is difficult to validate; latency variance may exceed design margin.
Risk: Dual-path redundancy increases complexity and potential for synchronization issues.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-C is sound but requires explicit sub-DPs for (1) network architecture (dedicated backbone + public fallback), (2) software real-time scheduling (priority queues, lock-free buffers), (3) latency monitoring and failover logic, and (4) operator interface for latency awareness. These should be decomposed in the next level.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency requirement. It encompasses telemetry acquisition and transmission (sensor-to-operator), command reception and transmission (operator-to-actuator), latency monitoring, and failover logic. It interfaces with Vehicle (for sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (for failsafe coordination), and Network Link (for communication).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Telemetry stream from Vehicle sensors (position, orientation, video, diagnostics) transmitted to operator; latency-critical path.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) received from remote operator; latency-critical path.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional: Teleoperation reports latency degradation and failover events to Safety Services; Safety Services may command failsafe state transitions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication over dedicated backbone and public internet; Teleoperation monitors link quality and switches paths.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system; teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the only one. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse latency ownership across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, Network). Not recommended.

Vehicle owns sensor acquisition and actuator control, but does not own operator-side processing, network infrastructure, or failover logic. Latency requirement spans beyond Vehicle scope. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation module is explicitly designed for remote operation. It owns end-to-end latency, dual-path network management, latency monitoring, and failover. All sub-DPs (network architecture, real-time scheduling, monitoring, failover) naturally map to Teleoperation. Excellent containment.

Safety Services owns failsafe state transitions and safety monitoring, but does not own latency measurement, network management, or operator interface. Latency is a safety input to Safety Services, not owned by it.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural and optimal owner of CR-NGHE-008. It has clear scope boundaries (remote operation infrastructure), owns all critical latency-affecting components (network paths, real-time scheduling, monitoring), and interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (failsafe coordination), and Network Link (communication substrate). Latency is a primary design driver for Teleoperation; diffusion to other modules would fragment ownership and accountability.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may not own the Vehicle-side sensor acquisition timing; if Vehicle introduces latency, Teleoperation cannot fully control end-to-end latency. Mitigation: Define clear latency budget allocation between Vehicle and Teleoperation in sub-FR decomposition.

Risk: Network Link module may introduce latency variance beyond Teleoperation's control (e.g., ISP routing changes). Mitigation: Teleoperation must monitor and adapt; failover logic must be robust to network variability.
Risk: Safety Services failsafe logic may introduce latency (e.g., state machine transitions). Mitigation: Coordinate with Safety Services to ensure failsafe latency is within acceptable bounds.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign CR-NGHE-008 to Teleoperation module. Define explicit latency budget allocation in sub-FRs: Vehicle sensor-to-Teleoperation ingestion (e.g., 10 ms), Teleoperation processing and transmission (e.g., 40 ms), network transit (e.g., 25 ms), operator rendering (e.g., 20 ms); command path similarly budgeted. Establish interface contracts with Vehicle and Safety Services to ensure latency contributions are measurable and traceable.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid dual-path architecture) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms p95). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach: dedicated backbone for critical paths, public internet fallback, real-time scheduling, and failover logic. The DP is implementable, testable, and traceable to the FR. No FR-DP mismatch detected.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Vehicle for sensor latency budgeting; ensure Vehicle-side timing is contractually defined.
Teleoperation module must coordinate with Safety Services for failsafe latency; ensure failover does not introduce unacceptable delays.
Teleoperation module depends on Network Link module for dual-path infrastructure; ensure Network Link provides the required interfaces and monitoring capabilities.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the clear owner of DP-C. All sub-DPs (network architecture, real-time scheduling, monitoring, failover) are naturally contained within Teleoperation scope. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. No diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-008 is verifiable via instrumented end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with Vehicle and Safety Services mocked) and in integration. Acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is objective and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Latency requirement is clearly allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) provide further allocation to sensor-to-operator, operator-to-actuator, degradation detection, and failsafe paths. Allocation is traceable and testable.

recommended_revisions: Decompose CR-NGHE-008 into sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) and assign each to specific Teleoperation sub-modules or interfaces.

Define explicit latency budget allocation: Vehicle (10 ms), Teleoperation ingestion/processing (40 ms), network transit (25 ms), operator rendering (20 ms), command path (55 ms). Ensure budgets sum to ≤ 150 ms with margin.
Establish interface contracts with Vehicle and Safety Services specifying latency contributions and monitoring requirements.
Plan verification strategy: unit tests for real-time scheduling, integration tests for dual-path failover, system tests for end-to-end latency under representative network conditions.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is a complex, non-atomic requirement spanning multiple sub-systems (sensor acquisition, network transmission, operator interface, failsafe logic). The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the IM assignment to Teleoperation is well-justified. However, the requirement must be decomposed into atomic sub-FRs to enable detailed design and verification. The four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) address distinct latency-critical paths and failsafe behavior; each requires its own DP-IM assignment. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to achieve implementable, verifiable requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, diagnostics) and transmit to remote operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall receive operator commands and transmit to vehicle actuators with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (latency, packet loss, link failure) to operator and safety services within 50 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall implement failsafe behavior (e.g., controlled stop, safe state) if end-to-end latency exceeds 200 ms for >2 seconds.

assumptions: Dedicated network backbone (5G private or fiber) can be provisioned at all operational sites.
Vehicle and operator station hardware support real-time processing with deterministic scheduling.
Operator training includes failover behavior and latency degradation awareness.
Network conditions are agreed upon and measurable (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Safety Services module can coordinate failsafe state transitions with Teleoperation within acceptable latency bounds.
open_risks: Software real-time scheduling is difficult to validate; latency variance may exceed design margin.
Dual-path redundancy increases complexity; synchronization issues may arise.
If dedicated backbone is unavailable at a site, system falls back to public internet; latency may exceed 150 ms.
Vehicle-side sensor acquisition latency is not fully under Teleoperation control; must be contractually managed.
Network Link module may introduce latency variance beyond Teleoperation's control.
evidence_needed: Network architecture design document specifying dedicated backbone and fallback paths.
Real-time scheduling design and validation (e.g., worst-case execution time analysis, jitter measurements).
Latency budget allocation document with traceability to sub-FRs.
Interface contracts with Vehicle and Safety Services specifying latency contributions.
Verification plan for end-to-end latency measurement under representative network conditions.
Failover logic design and test cases for latency degradation scenarios.
